input.content:
Act as a research-level mathematical proof author iterating on a
written deliverable in an Author/Critic loop. You have produced an
initial version of three canonical files; a Critic has reviewed them.
Your job each round is to refine the files in response to the Critic's
findings.

You have two tools:
  - **code_interpreter**: a Python sandbox with TeX Live + standard
    scientific libs. Run ``pdflatex`` to verify compile, and use
    sympy or numpy for computational exploration, verification and
    sanity checks. The sandbox has NO outbound network — use
    web_search_preview for any external URL or paper.
  - **web_search_preview**: free-form web search.

Your workspace lives at ``/mnt/data/`` and contains three canonical
files. The user message lists, for each, a read-only input path
(holding the previous round's contents) and a canonical write path
(``/mnt/data/<name>``) where you must write any updates.

How to update a file:
  1. Read its current contents from the listed read-only path.
  2. Build the new contents.
  3. Write to the canonical write path with
     ``Path("/mnt/data/<name>").write_text(...)``. Do not write to
     ``.new``/``.bak``/sibling paths — only canonical write paths are
     picked up.
  4. Compile-check answer.tex with ``pdflatex`` before claiming
     readiness.

A file you do not write at the canonical write path is treated as
unchanged this round.

**``research_notes.tex`` is your persistent scratchpad across rounds.**
Accumulate definitions, intermediate lemmas, computational sanity-checks,
alternative-approach sketches, dead ends you ruled out, literature notes
— anything you'd want to refer back to in a later round. **Do not turn
it into a changelog or a reply to the Critic** — that is what your
free-text thinking summary is for. A future Author turn should be able
to read research_notes.tex and pick up where it left off.

You may also emit three control blocks in your reply text:

  - **<council>...question...</council>** — invoke the Advisory
    Council on a specific sub-question. At most one per round. Use
    the Council when you are stuck or want a different angle: point
    it at specific side-problems or issues uncovered by the Critic,
    ask it to suggest a portfolio of potential approaches, or get a
    second opinion on a strategic choice. The primary purpose is
    *not* additional verification — that is handled in the parallel
    Critic call. Council members are independent strong models
    (Pro, Opus, Gemini); their replies arrive at the start of your
    next turn.

  - **<compute_agent>...instructions...</compute_agent>** —
    commission an out-of-band codex CLI worker for one focused task
    per round. At most one per round. Soft timeout 60 min, full
    network access, persistent workspace where ``code/``, ``data/``,
    ``papers/``, ``notes/`` survive across rounds, and — depending
    on the sandbox image — possibly a richer CAS toolchain (e.g.
    SageMath, GAP, Singular, PARI/GP). The worker can probe what is actually
    installed, so you don't need to know in advance; ask it to
    verify availability before committing to a particular tool.
    Best use is heavy computation or multi-round code development
    that would be too slow or too large for your own code_interpreter
    (which is ephemeral, has no network, and times out per cell),
    symbolic-algebra work that benefits from a real CAS rather than
    ``sympy``, or deeper literature retrieval (downloading actual
    PDFs / TeX sources from arXiv etc.). Each invocation the worker
    re-sees a fresh read-only snapshot of your three canonical
    files; it writes findings to
    ``responses/response_round_N.md`` and the whole workspace is
    zipped and attached to your *next* turn as a read-only file you
    can ``unzip`` via code_interpreter to inspect logs, data, and
    downloaded papers. The reply arrives at the start of your next
    turn, alongside any council replies. Give it specific, ordered,
    executable instructions — it does not see prior loop history
    beyond what you tell it and what is in its own persistent
    workspace.

  - **<ready>true</ready>** — signal that you believe the answer is
    ready for submission. See the readiness rules below.

Anything outside those control blocks is treated as a brief free-text
"thinking summary" for the human reviewer and the Critic. Do NOT
paste file contents in your reply.

---

Research ambition and problem interpretation. Your goal is to resolve
the given question to the fullest extent possible, with the ideal
outcome being a complete and rigorous solution. The solution may
require novel, creative, and non-trivial elements. If a non-trivial
lemma, computation, or reduction appears necessary, attempt to prove
the lemma, run the computation, or carry out the reduction in this turn
before listing it as a gap. Do not stop at "this remains to be proved"
while you still have a plausible route to attack it.

If the problem statement is ambiguous, inconsistent, missing
definitions, or likely contains a typo, begin answer.tex with a short
section titled "Problem statement and interpretation". State the
cleanest faithful correction or restatement, list any assumptions, and
do not silently solve a different problem. Record the same
interpretation in research_notes.tex so later rounds preserve it. If
the problem is already well-defined, no extra interpretation section is
needed.

When open gaps remain in your treatment, end answer.tex with a section
titled "Remaining open issues" (or equivalent). For each gap, state:
(a) what it is mathematically; (b) where in the document it appears;
(c) what was tried this round or earlier; (d) what would be needed to
close it (a specific computation, an alternative approach, a missing
reference, etc.). If rounds remain and any essential mathematical gap
is still open, keep working by focusing on closing that gap or
exploring an alternative approach to the problem.

Readiness rule. Declare ``<ready>true</ready>`` only when you
genuinely believe the answer is a complete rigorous solution to the
stated problem, with no remaining open gaps, no unproved essential
lemmas, and no missing assumptions. Do not declare
``<ready>true</ready>`` merely because the last round has been reached
or because a "Remaining open issues" section honestly lists what is
left; partial final outputs may list open issues, but they are not
ready. Even then the run terminates only if the Critic also concurs.

### Problem ###
Let $G=\langle a_1,\dots,a_r\rangle$ be a free group. A word on the
  letters $L=\{a_1,a_1^{-1},\dots,a_r,a_r^{-1}\}$ is called reducible
  if it equals $1\in G$. A word is called quasi-reduced if it does not
  contain three consecutive letters $\ell\ell^{-1}\ell$ for any
  $\ell \in L$.

  Fix a reducible quasi-reduced word $w=w_1\cdots w_{2n}$ (with $w_i\in L$). A
  matching $M$ consists of $n$ intervals, each properly embedded in
  $\RR\times\RR_+$, satisfying the following:
  \begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
  \item Any two intervals intersect
    transversally, and there are no triple points.
  \item $\bdy M=\{1,\dots,2n\}\times 0\subset\RR\times 0\subset
    \RR\times\RR_+$.
  \item For each component $R$ of
    $\RR\times\RR_+\setminus M$, and for each component $A$ of $M\cap \bdy\overline{R}$, $\bdy A$ consists of some two points $(i,0)$ and $(j,0)$ with $w_i=w_j^{-1}$. 
  \end{itemize}
  A $k$-crossing
  matching is a matching where the total number of self-intersections
  is $k$.

  Let $F_w$ be the following CW complex whose $k$-cells correspond to
  isotopy classes of $k$-crossing matchings. Given a $k$-crossing
  matching $M$ with crossing set $X(M)$, the associated $k$-cell is
  given by $C(M)\defeq \prod_{c\in X(M)}I_c$, where $I_c$ is an
  interval whose endpoints correspond to the two ways of locally
  resolving $M$ near $c$. The attaching maps are given as follows: On
  a facet of the form
  \[
    a\times\prod_{d\in X(M)\setminus\{c\}}I_{d},\qquad c\in X(M),a\in \bdy I_c,
  \]
  let $M_a$ be the $(k-1)$-crossing matching obtained by resolving $M$
  at $c$ according to $a$. Then the $(k-1)$-cell associated to $M_a$
  is given by
  $C(M_a)=\prod_{d\in X(M_a)}I_d=\prod_{d\in X(M)\setminus\{c\}}I_{d}$, and the
  above facet maps to this $(k-1)$-cell canonically.

  Is $F_w$ contractible?

### Round 1 of 5 ###
Budget used so far: $22.49 / $1000.00

### First Proof LaTeX contract ###
First Proof LaTeX submission contract:
- `answer.tex` must be a complete standalone LaTeX document.
- Use exactly `\documentclass[12pt]{article}`.
- The compiled PDF must be at most 12 pages.
- The `fullpage` package is permitted.
- Do not use any other margin/layout changes: no `geometry`, `a4wide`,
  `typearea`, `anysize`, manual `\textwidth`, `\textheight`,
  `\oddsidemargin`, `\evensidemargin`, `\topmargin`, etc.
- Do not change line spacing: no `setspace`, `\linespread`,
  `\baselinestretch`, `\onehalfspacing`, or `\doublespacing`.
- Do not change font size inside the document: no `\small`,
  `\footnotesize`, `\scriptsize`, or `\fontsize`.
- Run `pdflatex` and fix compile errors before claiming readiness.


### Workspace files ###
- answer.tex: read from `/mnt/data/file-6gXajTSv321ozXGW7CUQFF-answer.tex` (read-only); write your edited version to `/mnt/data/answer.tex`.
- research_notes.tex: read from `/mnt/data/file-1TT6MkF9oEh9gAVsmqJvbH-research_notes.tex` (read-only); write your edited version to `/mnt/data/research_notes.tex`.
- references.bib: read from `/mnt/data/file-1ZQF89LqpK9fgkaMneCpD7-references.bib` (read-only); write your edited version to `/mnt/data/references.bib`.

### Latest Critic review ###
## Referee report

### Overall verdict

I find the submitted `answer.tex` mathematically valid. It gives a concrete quasi-reduced reducible word \(w\) for which the associated complex \(F_w\) is homeomorphic to \(S^2\), hence not contractible. I found no fatal mathematical gap, no unresolved open issue, and no LaTeX-contract violation.

There are a few places where the exposition is compressed, especially the “innermost cycle” argument and the final identification with \(S^2\), but the missing details are standard and do not appear to conceal a mathematical error.

---

## LaTeX contract check

- `\documentclass[12pt]{article}` is used exactly.
- `fullpage` is used, which is permitted.
- No forbidden margin/layout package or manual layout changes appear.
- No line-spacing changes appear.
- No in-document font-size changes such as `\small`, `\footnotesize`, `\fontsize`, etc. appear. The use of `{\bf ...}` is obsolete LaTeX style but not a font-size change.
- I ran `pdflatex`; compilation succeeded.
- The compiled PDF is 2 pages, within the 12-page limit.

No LaTeX-contract issue found.

---

## Literature / web check

`answer.tex` contains no citation commands, and `references.bib` is empty. Thus there are no cited literature results to validate. The proof is self-contained.

---

## Code-interpreter cross-checks

I independently enumerated perfect matchings of six cyclically ordered endpoints and computed the crossing numbers. The distribution is:

\[
\#\{0\text{-crossing}\}=5,\quad
\#\{1\text{-crossing}\}=6,\quad
\#\{2\text{-crossing}\}=3,\quad
\#\{3\text{-crossing}\}=1.
\]

The unique three-crossing pairing is indeed

\[
14|25|36.
\]

The five vertices, six edges, and three squares listed in `answer.tex` match this enumeration. I also checked the local resolutions of the three square cells; the stated boundaries are correct.

The resulting cellular complex has one-skeleton a theta graph with three length-two paths and three square 2-cells filling the three cycles. Its cellular homology is consistent with \(S^2\): \(H_0\cong \mathbb Z\), \(H_1=0\), \(H_2\cong \mathbb Z\).

---

## Paragraph-by-paragraph audit

### Counterexample word

The proposed word is

\[
w=\bar a\,a\,b\,\bar b\,\bar a\,a\,c\,\bar c\,\bar a\,a.
\]

This is freely trivial by deleting the adjacent inverse pairs

\[
\bar a a,\quad b\bar b,\quad \bar a a,\quad c\bar c,\quad \bar a a.
\]

The quasi-reduced condition is also correctly checked: the only adjacent inverse pairs are the displayed ones, and after each such pair the next letter, when present, is not the first letter of the pair. Thus no subword of the form \(\ell\ell^{-1}\ell\) occurs.

No issue.

---

### Definition of \(\Gamma(M)\)

The compactification of the upper half-plane to a disk and the conversion of crossings into graph vertices are standard. Boundary endpoints have valence one; interior crossing vertices have valence four.

No issue.

---

### Lemma 1: \(\Gamma(M)\) is a forest

The argument is essentially correct. If \(\Gamma(M)\) had a graph-theoretic cycle, the cycle cannot pass through a boundary endpoint because boundary endpoints have valence one. Hence it lies in the interior of the disk. An innermost such cycle gives a complementary face whose \(M\)-boundary contains a closed component, or at least a component with no boundary endpoint, violating the rule that every component \(A\subset M\cap \partial\overline R\) has two boundary endpoints with inverse labels.

The proof is slightly compressed: one should explicitly justify that an innermost cycle bounds a complementary region, i.e. that no branches inside the cycle obstruct this. But in a finite embedded graph with all leaves on the disk boundary, any interior branch inside an innermost cycle would either have to terminate illegally in the interior or return to the cycle and create a smaller cycle. So the argument is sound.

No fatal issue.

---

### Lemma 2: unique inverse-letter pair gives an uncrossed interval

The lemma states that if \(x\) and \(x^{-1}\) each occur exactly once, then those endpoints are joined by an uncrossed interval. The proof uses Lemma 1 to know the relevant graph component is a tree.

The local-side argument at the first crossing is correct: the two local face-boundary sides of the incoming edge leave the crossing through two different half-edges. Removing the crossing vertex separates those half-edges into different components of the tree, so at most one can contain the unique inverse-labelled endpoint \(q\). But the label condition forces both local face-boundary components beginning at \(p\) to end at \(q\), contradiction. Therefore the path from \(p\) to \(q\) contains no crossing and is a single uncrossed interval.

No issue.

---

### Deleting the forced \(b\bar b\) and \(c\bar c\) intervals

The application of Lemma 2 to the unique \(b,\bar b\) and \(c,\bar c\) occurrences is correct. The intervals \((3,4)\) and \((7,8)\) are forced and uncrossed.

Because their endpoints are adjacent on the boundary, each such interval is boundary-parallel and cuts off an empty disk. Deleting them preserves crossing data and gives a cell-preserving bijection with the analogous complex for

\[
u=\bar a\,a\,\bar a\,a\,\bar a\,a.
\]

The word \(u\) is not quasi-reduced, but that is not a problem: it is only being used as an auxiliary shorter labelled-boundary problem with the same matching definition.

No issue.

---

### Classification of matchings for \(u\)

For \(u\), odd endpoints have label \(\bar a\), even endpoints have label \(a\). The proof correctly observes that with three intervals:

- no two intervals can cross twice, since two crossings between the same pair would create a cycle in \(\Gamma(M)\);
- not all three pairs can cross, since three pairwise crossings create a cycle in the resulting graph.

Thus the only excluded six-endpoint pairing is the unique all-crossing pairing \(14|25|36\).

The statement that the isotopy class is determined by the endpoint pairing is standard for these forest chord diagrams. It could be expanded, but in the six-endpoint case it is harmless and correct.

No fatal issue.

---

### Converse parity argument

The converse argument says that if the interleaving graph is a forest, then every face-boundary component connects endpoints of opposite parity. The pushed-off-arc argument is valid:

- a face-boundary component can be pushed slightly into its adjacent face to obtain an arc disjoint from the diagram;
- cutting along that arc separates the marked endpoints into two sets;
- no interval can have endpoints on opposite sides, or it would cross the pushed-off arc;
- hence each side contains an even number of marked endpoints;
- therefore the two endpoints of the face-boundary component have opposite parity.

For the alternating word \(u\), opposite parity is exactly the inverse-label condition. So every forest pairing is admissible.

No issue.

---

### Enumeration of cells

The listed cells are correct.

Vertices:

\[
12|34|56,\quad 12|36|45,\quad 14|23|56,\quad
16|23|45,\quad 16|25|34.
\]

Edges:

\[
12|35|46,\quad 13|24|56,\quad 13|26|45,\quad
15|23|46,\quad 15|26|34,\quad 16|24|35.
\]

Squares:

\[
13|25|46,\quad 14|26|35,\quad 15|24|36.
\]

This matches the independent enumeration.

No issue.

---

### Boundary maps of square cells

The stated square boundaries are correct. For example, resolving the two crossings of \(13|25|46\) gives exactly

\[
12|35|46,\quad 15|23|46,\quad 13|24|56,\quad 13|26|45.
\]

The other two square boundaries also check out.

No issue.

---

### Topological identification with \(S^2\)

The one-skeleton is indeed a theta graph with poles \(v_0\) and \(v_3\), and with three length-two paths through \(v_1,v_2,v_4\). The three square cells fill the three cycles determined by pairs of those paths.

This is the usual three-lune cell decomposition of \(S^2\). Equivalently, the complex is a closed connected 2-dimensional cell complex with Euler characteristic

\[
5-6+3=2
\]

and the local links are circles. Thus it is homeomorphic to \(S^2\).

Therefore \(F_w\cong S^2\), so \(F_w\) is not contractible.

No issue.

---

## Final assessment

The solution successfully gives a counterexample and proves non-contractibility. I found no mathematical error or LaTeX-contract violation that would prevent readiness.

### Workflow compile/format feedback ###
No LaTeX compile or formatting issues detected.

### Advisory Council replies (if you requested any last turn) ###
(empty)

### Compute worker reply (if you commissioned one last turn) ###
(empty)

Edit the canonical files via code_interpreter cells as described in
the system prompt. Optionally invoke ``<council>`` on at most one
specific sub-question, and/or ``<compute_agent>`` on at most one
focused computation/code/literature task, or set
``<ready>true</ready>`` if you believe the answer is ready for
submission. Do NOT paste the canonical files in your reply.

summary.text:
**Analyzing region boundaries**

I'm considering the boundaries of a region R, which is a component of UHP \ M. It seems the boundary could pass along segments of M and turn through crossings. I think A is defined by certain boundary points, which need to create connected arcs from one boundary point to another without ending at crossings.

So, at crossings, the boundary seems to pass through, but it needs a model. M is immersed arcs, and M∩∂\bar R might travel along one side of the arcs, but both arcs at a crossing belong to M and are in the boundary too. This subset might include crossings and several edges but should be closed without branching.

**Understanding graph conditions**

I'm considering how conditions within a graph exclude cycles, ensuring that local resolution cells are present. In the original proof's Lemma 1, it states that the graph formed by using crossings as vertices is a forest. I need to examine if this property implies no cycles.

If there's a cycle, I would choose the innermost one, which would bound a disk and possibly have branches inside. I'm curious if, in this case, the boundary region could include some parts of the cycle along with these branches. 

Could the "M∩∂R component" have endpoints, and can I formalize this? A finite graph embedded in a disk, where all leaves are on the boundary with crossing values, would be interesting. I wonder if an innermost cycle always bounds an open disk that contains no graph points, especially without interior leaves.

**Examining cycles and trees in graphs**

I'm thinking about branches inside a graph that could be attached to cycle C. If they end at the boundary, that's impossible since the boundary is outside of C. I wonder if they could connect at two vertices on C, forming smaller cycles, or possibly each having one connection at an interior vertex, but all interior vertices have degree four.

If the finite tree is connected to C, its leaves must be either on C or interior to it. But if there’s only one attachment and no interior leaves, that’s tricky because a finite tree should have more than one leaf. I think the attachment vertices on C shouldn't be leaves in the entire graph context.

In the subgraph, leaves could all be on C, which could create a chord or path between two vertices, forming a smaller cycle. If there's a theta shape inside, that also indicates cycles, which leads me to choose the minimal cycle by inclusion, ensuring the disk inside contains no other graph points apart from C itself.

**Exploring region boundaries and intersections**

I'm thinking about the inside region R and its boundary C, which could be a Jordan curve made up of graph edges. I wonder if M∩∂R equals C, but since A is closed and has no boundary endpoints, that seems like a violation — which is good to note. 

Now I need to examine Lemma 2. It mentions unique inverse pairs, suggesting that endpoints are connected by an uncrossed interval. If p's incident interval heads towards a crossing, the local side from p might follow one of two possible outgoing half-edges. 

However, the local face-boundary component A serves as a topological arc in M∩∂R with defined endpoints, including point p and some unique endpoint marked as inverse. Since q is unique, both sides must end at q. The prior lemma established that the graph component acts as a tree, meaning the path from p to q in the graph is unique — though that path may not be the interval itself; instead, it could follow an edge path.

**Analyzing crossing paths**

I'm thinking about what happens if a path meets the first crossing x. The two face-boundary arcs from point p would follow both sides of the same edge to x, then exit through different half-edges. I’m curious why those half-edges are different.

At a transverse crossing of two arcs, I envision four sectors. The side above one edge leading to p forms the boundary of an adjacent region. Then at the crossing, it continues along one of the crossing-arc half-edges, while the other side of the incoming edge takes the opposite half-edge.

I can picture it this way: there are four half-edges: the incoming edge from p, the continuation of that edge past the crossing, and two other half-edges f and g. The boundaries of the two regions on either side of edge e might continue along f and g at the crossing.

Depending on the quadrant, it seems like the boundary shifts to follow the crossing arc instead of going straight through the continuation beyond the crossing. That’s because the complement boundary has to turn around the crossing to avoid intersections.

**Examining edge crossings in trees**

I'm considering how if the path leaves via two different half-edges from the crossing, removing the crossing vertex in a tree separates all incident half-edges into distinct components. In a tree, yes, if I remove a vertex, each incident edge now belongs to a distinct component, and at most one can contain q. But both face components should end at q, leading to a contradiction. So, there shouldn't be any crossings on the path.

This suggests that p is connected to q by a path without crossings. Since the vertices are just boundary endpoints and crossings, it seems like the path could be one whole interval. I need to check if p and q might be the endpoints of the same interval. 

Now, the graph component path from p to q should have no internal crossing edges, which would mean that the graph edges are just intervals between boundary and crossing vertices. If there are no crossings, then there should be an edge with endpoints p and q.

Finally, there’s a mention of forced intervals for a word of length 10, where endpoints 3 and 4—let’s call them b and b-bar—are adjacent. The lemma indicates those endpoints are connected by an uncrossed interval. If they are adjacent, would that mean they are boundary-parallel?

**Evaluating embedded arcs and crossings**

I’m thinking about how an uncrossed embedded arc that connects adjacent boundary points might not be boundary-parallel because it could wrap around other arcs. In a disk, if an embedded arc has its endpoints adjacent on the boundary, it must be disjoint from all other arcs. By the Jordan curve theorem, the boundary segment between these endpoints should create an area without any marked endpoints inside, effectively cutting off a disk.

But could this disk contain closed components of other arcs? Since all interval endpoints are on the boundary, it seems there's no way. So, I conclude it should be an empty disk. 

When deleting gives an isomorphism, I need to ensure that two forced intervals do not cross each other, meaning an "uncrossed interval" has no crossings at all. This makes sense; a path should have no crossings too. So, they should be boundary-parallel.

Now F_w is isomorphic to the analogous structure for u, but I need to be cautious since the definition was originally intended for a reducible quasi-reduced word. Is u considered quasi-reduced? I suspect there's an analogous labeling issue. And, is the cell-preserving bijection precise enough?

**Examining matchings and intervals**

I’m considering how all matchings for w include forced intervals, and after deleting, the remaining arcs should form a matching for u while satisfying certain conditions. I need to look at the regions adjacent to the deleted arcs; since these regions are boundary-parallel with an empty disk, the local conditions in the remaining diagram should correspond.

Now, if I have a matching for u, could I insert two boundary-parallel arcs at positions 3, 4 and 7, 8? In the original boundary order, inserting the pairs b, b-bar and c, c-bar would cut the empty disk, leaving the rest unaffected. 

Next, I need to classify u. Here, the labels alternate: odd ones are \bar a and even ones are a. The original definition's label condition for each component of M∩boundary requires inverse labels. We characterize admissibility by interleaving within a graph forest.

One important thing to ensure is that, based on the first lemma, with three intervals, no two can cross twice, and not all three pairs can cross. An isotopy class is then determined by the endpoint pairing, having crossings precisely when two pairs interleave. I should unpack this further.

**Analyzing endpoint reductions and matchings**

I’m working with six endpoints and three intervals. A matching consists of embedded intervals that can cross pairwise at most. According to the lemma regarding forests, if the graph has no cycles, then two intervals crossing twice would form a cycle (a bigon), which is impossible.

If all three pairs cross exactly once, I get a triangle cycle with three crossings, making it also impossible. What about two non-interleaving intervals crossing once? Since arcs in a disk with non-interleaving endpoints have an even crossing count, the forest excludes two or more crossings, so there should be zero crossings there.

For interleaving endpoints, the crossing count is odd, but the forest excludes more than one crossing. Actually, two intervals that interleave could have one, three, or more crossings. But having two crossings would create a cycle! So, I conclude that there can only be exactly one crossing.

**Determining crossing through endpoints**

I'm exploring how crossing is defined by endpoint pairing and thinking about using parity modulo 2. This might seem omitted, but I want to clarify that a crossing occurs exactly when intervals interleave, relying on standard parity and allowing at most one crossing. 

If I have a chord diagram with a crossing for interleaving pairs (and no crossings for non-interleaving), the question arises: does the interleaving graph being a forest imply the chord diagram's graph is also a forest? 

If the interleaving graph is a forest, each pair of intervals crosses at most once. This graph could potentially have a path with two crossings, but no cycles. With three chords forming a crossing graph, it looks similar to a tree, and if this crossing graph has no cycles, what are the general implications?

**Analyzing interval crossings**

I'm considering how three intervals interact: if all three pairwise cross, that creates a triangle cycle, while fewer crossings would indicate a forest structure. If two intervals cross and the third only crosses one of them, the graph can still be tree-like without cycles. 

I need to examine every face-boundary component, ensuring they don't close up. In a forest, each component would ideally be an arc with endpoints at the leaves. This allows me to assert that the parity relation ensures inverse labels are applied.

There’s a question about component A of \( M \cap \overline{R} \); if this graph is a forest with alternating endpoint labels of opposite parity, that’s acceptable. Then I should enumerate the cells in \( F_u \), ensuring to account for all 14 pairings, excluding the cases 14|25|36, based on crossing numbers.

**Calculating crossing counts**

I need to compute the crossing count based on the number of edges in the interleaving graph. For noncrossing perfect matchings with 6 points, that's equivalent to Catalan 5 vertices. If there's one crossing, I get 6 edges, and for a two-crossing path, I'm looking at 3 squares. All-crossing triangles get excluded, which is good. 

I'm considering these "squares" as 2-dimensional cells. Their boundaries get resolved by addressing the crossings. It's important to note that there are no 3-cells because all-crossing cases are excluded. So, \( F_w \cong F_u \) has cells with dimensions up to 2, which is helpful for my topological identification.

Now, I want to verify the edges in my one-skeleton theta graph with poles \( v_0 \) and \( v_3 \) and three length-two paths. Okay, so I have the edges: \( v_0-v_1 \) as \( e_{01} \); \( v_1-v_3 \) as \( e_{13} \); \( v_0-v_2 \) as \( e_{02} \); \( v_2-v_3 \) as \( e_{23} \); \( v_0-v_4 \) as \( e_{04} \); and \( v_4-v_3 \) as \( e_{34} \).

For the squares, \( Q_{12} \) has paths through \( v_1 \) and \( v_2 \), filling the cycle \( v_0-v_1-v_3-v_2-v_0 \). Square \( Q_{14} \) takes paths through \( v_1 \) and \( v_4 \), while \( Q_{24} \) goes through \( v_2 \) and \( v_4 \). I'll need to attach three disks along each of these pairs of paths.

This leads me to wonder if, topologically, it all represents \( S^2 \). I must be careful to ensure that I correctly attach disks to the theta graph along all three cycles. Lastly, I’m also considering Euler's formula and how that relates to what I'm grouping together.

**Exploring homeomorphisms and disks**

I’m considering whether vertices can form a local link in a circle. We can demonstrate an explicit homeomorphism starting with one square (disk) attached along the cycle \( P_1 \cup P_2 \). Then, I attach a second square along \( P_1 \cup P_3 \), glued along the path \( P_1 \). The union of these two disks creates a disk with a boundary of \( P_2 \cup P_3 \). 

These two squares connect at a common boundary, sharing edges \( e_{01} \) and \( e_{13} \), leading to a single disk. The third square is attached along this boundary, resulting in \( S^2 \). This is a more rigorous approach that avoids local links. 

I think I don't need to include the proof about "isotopy class determined by endpoint pairing." Instead, I’ll note that for two arcs, crossing parity equals interleaving, and a forest of three arcs has no additional isotopy data.

**Addressing forest diagrams and homeomorphisms**

However, I need to consider the potential issue of whether \( F_w \) is homeomorphic to \( S^2 \) versus a CW complex created from product cells with attaching maps.

from pathlib import Path
answer = r"""\documentclass[12pt]{article}
\usepackage{fullpage}
\usepackage{amsmath,amssymb,amsthm}

\newtheorem{lemma}{Lemma}
\newcommand{\ol}{\overline}

\begin{document}

\begin{center}
\textbf{A counterexample}
\end{center}

The answer is no.  We give a counterexample in
\(G=\langle a,b,c\rangle\).  Write \(\ol x=x^{-1}\), and set
\[
        w=\ol a\,a\, b\,\ol b\,\ol a\,a\, c\,\ol c\,\ol a\,a .
\]
This word is freely trivial, by deleting the adjacent inverse pairs
\[
        \ol a a,\qquad b\ol b,\qquad \ol a a,\qquad c\ol c,\qquad
        \ol a a .
\]
It is quasi-reduced: the only adjacent inverse pairs are the five
displayed ones, and in each case the following letter, if there is one,
is not the first letter of that pair.

We shall use two elementary consequences of the face condition in the
definition.  Compactify the upper half-plane to a disk and put the
marked boundary points in cyclic order.  For a matching \(M\), let
\(\Gamma(M)\) be the graph obtained from \(M\) by making each crossing a
vertex; the original boundary endpoints are leaves.

\begin{lemma}
For every admissible matching \(M\), the graph \(\Gamma(M)\) is a
forest.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
Suppose that \(\Gamma(M)\) contains a cycle.  Boundary vertices have
valence one, so every cycle is contained in the interior of the disk.
Choose one which is innermost: the disk bounded by it contains no other
cycle.  It in fact contains no edge of \(\Gamma(M)\).  For if a finite
subgraph lay inside and met the chosen cycle, then either it would
contain a path joining two vertices of the cycle, producing a smaller
cycle, or it would contain a tree with an interior leaf, impossible since
all interior vertices have valence four and all leaves of
\(\Gamma(M)\) lie on the boundary of the disk.

Thus one complementary region has this cycle as a boundary component.
The corresponding component of
\(M\cap\partial\overline R\) is closed and has no boundary endpoint.
This contradicts the defining requirement that every such component
have boundary consisting of two marked boundary points with inverse
labels.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}
Suppose that a letter \(x\) occurs in the word exactly once and
\(x^{-1}\) occurs exactly once.  Then, in every admissible matching, the
two corresponding endpoints are joined by an uncrossed interval.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
Let the two endpoints be \(p\) and \(q\).  At \(p\), the two local sides
of the incident edge belong to two components of face boundary.  By the
label condition, both of these components must end at \(q\), since
\(q\) is the only endpoint with the inverse label.

By the preceding lemma the component of \(\Gamma(M)\) containing \(p\)
is a tree, and hence there is a unique graph path from \(p\) to \(q\).
If this path met a crossing, let \(c\) be the first crossing encountered
when travelling from \(p\).  At \(c\), the two local face-boundary sides
of the incoming edge leave along two different half-edges.  Removing
\(c\) from the tree separates those two half-edges into different
components, so at most one of the two resulting face-boundary arcs can
continue to \(q\).  This contradicts the previous paragraph.  Therefore
the path from \(p\) to \(q\) has no crossing, and is a single uncrossed
interval.
\end{proof}

In the word \(w\), the letters \(b,\ol b,c,\ol c\) each occur only in
the displayed adjacent inverse pairs.  Hence every admissible matching
contains the uncrossed intervals \((3,4)\) and \((7,8)\).  Since their
endpoints are adjacent on the boundary, these intervals cut off empty
boundary disks.  Deleting them and renumbering the remaining endpoints
\(1,2,5,6,9,10\) gives a cell-preserving isomorphism from \(F_w\) to the
analogous six-endpoint complex for
\[
        u=\ol a\,a\,\ol a\,a\,\ol a\,a .
\]
Here \(u\) is only an auxiliary labelled boundary word; the same matching
definition is being used after the forced intervals have been removed.

We now compute this six-endpoint complex.  For \(u\), odd endpoints have
label \(\ol a\) and even endpoints have label \(a\).  Write
\[
        ij|kl|mn
\]
for the pairing \(\{i,j\},\{k,l\},\{m,n\}\).  By Lemma 1, two intervals
cannot cross twice, since two crossings between them would make a bigon
cycle.  Also, all three pairs of intervals cannot cross, since the
three crossings would form a triangular cycle.  Hence the crossing
pattern is determined by the endpoint pairing: two pairs cross exactly
when their endpoints interleave around the boundary.

Conversely, take any pairing for which the interleaving graph is a
forest, and draw it with one transverse crossing for each interleaving
pair and no other crossings.  Then \(\Gamma(M)\) is a forest.  Every
component of \(M\cap\partial\overline R\) is therefore an arc joining
two boundary endpoints.  Push such an arc slightly into the adjacent
face.  The pushed-off arc is disjoint from the diagram and cuts the disk
into two pieces.  No interval can have its endpoints on different sides,
so each side contains an even number of marked endpoints.  Therefore
the two endpoints of the face-boundary arc have opposite parity.  For
the alternating word \(u\), opposite parity is exactly the inverse-label
condition.  Thus precisely the pairings with forest interleaving graph
are admissible.  Among the fifteen pairings of six points, the only
excluded one is the all-crossing pairing \(14|25|36\).

The resulting cells are the following.  The five vertices are
\[
\begin{array}{lll}
v_0=12|34|56, & v_1=12|36|45, & v_2=14|23|56,\\
v_3=16|23|45, & v_4=16|25|34. &
\end{array}
\]
The six edges are
\[
\begin{array}{lll}
e_{01}=12|35|46, & e_{02}=13|24|56, & e_{13}=13|26|45,\\
e_{23}=15|23|46, & e_{04}=15|26|34, & e_{34}=16|24|35,
\end{array}
\]
where the subscripts indicate the two endpoint vertices.  The three
squares are
\[
        Q_{12}=13|25|46,\qquad
        Q_{14}=14|26|35,\qquad
        Q_{24}=15|24|36,
\]
and their boundaries, obtained by the two local resolutions of each
crossing, are
\[
\begin{aligned}
\partial Q_{12}&=e_{01}\cup e_{13}\cup e_{23}\cup e_{02},\\
\partial Q_{14}&=e_{01}\cup e_{13}\cup e_{34}\cup e_{04},\\
\partial Q_{24}&=e_{02}\cup e_{23}\cup e_{34}\cup e_{04}.
\end{aligned}
\]

Thus the one-skeleton is the theta graph with poles \(v_0\) and \(v_3\)
and with three length-two paths through \(v_1,v_2,v_4\).  The square
\(Q_{12}\) fills the cycle formed by the paths through \(v_1\) and
\(v_2\).  The square \(Q_{14}\) fills the cycle formed by the paths
through \(v_1\) and \(v_4\).  These two squares are glued along the
common path through \(v_1\), so their union is a disk whose boundary is
the remaining cycle, the one through \(v_2\) and \(v_4\).  Finally
\(Q_{24}\) is glued along exactly this boundary.  Hence the whole
complex is a 2-sphere:
\[
        F_w \cong S^2 .
\]
In particular \(F_w\) is not contractible.

\end{document}
"""
Path("/mnt/data/answer.tex").write_text(answer)
print(len(answer), answer.count("\n"))

notes = r"""\documentclass{article}
\usepackage{amsmath,amssymb}
\begin{document}
Research notes for the matching-complex question.

Counterexample in rank \(3\):
\[
w=\bar a a\, b\bar b\, \bar a a\, c\bar c\, \bar a a.
\]
It is freely trivial by adjacent cancellations.  It is quasi-reduced
because the only adjacent inverse pairs are
\(\bar a a\), \(b\bar b\), \(\bar a a\), \(c\bar c\), \(\bar a a\);
after each such pair the next letter, when present, is not the first
letter of the pair.  The \(b\bar b\) and \(c\bar c\) pairs serve as
separators which prevent the shorter alternating word
\(\bar a a\bar a a\bar a a\) from violating quasi-reducedness.

Interpretation of the matching condition used throughout: compactify the
upper half-plane to a disk, put vertices at crossings, and regard
\(M\) as a finite planar graph \(\Gamma(M)\) whose boundary vertices are
the marked endpoints and whose interior vertices are transverse
crossings.  The condition in the problem says that each component of
\(M\cap\partial\overline R\) for a complementary region \(R\) is an arc
with both endpoints among the marked boundary points, and those labels
are inverse.

Structural lemmas.

1.  \(\Gamma(M)\) is a forest for every admissible matching.  Any graph
cycle is interior since boundary vertices are leaves.  Choose an
innermost cycle.  If there were graph inside its disk, then either a
path between two points of the cycle would create a smaller cycle or a
finite tree branch would have an illegal interior leaf (all interior
vertices have valence \(4\), all true leaves are boundary endpoints).
Thus the cycle itself is a boundary component of a complementary
region, giving a component of \(M\cap\partial\overline R\) with no
marked endpoints, contrary to the definition.

2.  If a letter \(x\) and its inverse each occur once, then their
endpoints are joined by an uncrossed interval.  Let the endpoints be
\(p,q\).  The two local sides of the incident edge at \(p\) must both
belong to face-boundary arcs ending at \(q\).  In the tree component of
\(\Gamma(M)\), take the unique path from \(p\) to \(q\).  At the first
crossing on that path, the two local sides of the incoming edge leave
along two different half-edges; after deleting the crossing vertex these
half-edges lie in different tree components, so at most one can contain
\(q\).  Contradiction.  Hence no crossing lies on the path, which is a
single interval.

3.  Applying (2) to \(b,\bar b\) and \(c,\bar c\) forces the intervals
\((3,4)\) and \((7,8)\).  Since the endpoints are adjacent, each
interval is boundary-parallel and cuts off an empty disk.  Deleting
these forced intervals identifies \(F_w\) cell-by-cell with the
analogous labelled-boundary complex for
\[
u=\bar a a\bar a a\bar a a
\]
on six endpoints.  The auxiliary word \(u\) need not be quasi-reduced;
it is just the residual labelled boundary problem.

Six-endpoint computation for \(u\).

Odd endpoints are labelled \(\bar a\), even endpoints \(a\).  There are
15 pairings.  In an admissible matching, two intervals cannot cross
twice (bigon cycle), and all three pairs cannot cross (triangle cycle).
Together with mod-2 crossing parity for chords in a disk, this means
the crossing pattern is determined by the endpoint pairing: two pairs
cross iff their endpoints interleave.  The unique all-crossing pairing
is \(14|25|36\), so it is excluded.

Conversely, for any pairing whose interleaving graph is a forest, draw
one crossing for each interleaving pair.  The resulting
\(\Gamma(M)\) is a forest.  Face-boundary components are arcs between
boundary endpoints.  Push one such arc slightly into its adjacent face;
the pushed arc is disjoint from the diagram and separates the marked
points into two sets.  No matching interval crosses the pushed arc, so
each side contains an even number of marked points.  Hence the endpoints
of the face-boundary arc have opposite parity, exactly the inverse-label
condition for \(u\).  Thus admissible pairings are precisely all
pairings except \(14|25|36\).

Cells for \(u\):
\[
\begin{array}{lll}
v_0=12|34|56, & v_1=12|36|45, & v_2=14|23|56,\\
v_3=16|23|45, & v_4=16|25|34; &
\end{array}
\]
\[
\begin{array}{lll}
e_{01}=12|35|46, & e_{02}=13|24|56, & e_{13}=13|26|45,\\
e_{23}=15|23|46, & e_{04}=15|26|34, & e_{34}=16|24|35;
\end{array}
\]
\[
Q_{12}=13|25|46,\qquad Q_{14}=14|26|35,\qquad Q_{24}=15|24|36.
\]
Boundaries:
\[
\partial Q_{12}=e_{01}\cup e_{13}\cup e_{23}\cup e_{02},\quad
\partial Q_{14}=e_{01}\cup e_{13}\cup e_{34}\cup e_{04},\quad
\partial Q_{24}=e_{02}\cup e_{23}\cup e_{34}\cup e_{04}.
\]
The 1-skeleton is a theta graph from \(v_0\) to \(v_3\) with middle
vertices \(v_1,v_2,v_4\).  \(Q_{12}\) and \(Q_{14}\) glued along the
path through \(v_1\) form a disk whose boundary is the cycle through
\(v_2,v_4\); \(Q_{24}\) glues to that boundary.  Therefore the complex
is \(S^2\), not contractible.

\end{document}
"""
Path("/mnt/data/research_notes.tex").write_text(notes)
Path("/mnt/data/references.bib").write_text(paths["bib"].read_text())
print(len(notes), len(paths["bib"].read_text()))

p=Path("/mnt/data/answer.tex")
text=p.read_text()
old = r"""Choose one which is innermost: the disk bounded by it contains no other
cycle.  It in fact contains no edge of \(\Gamma(M)\).  For if a finite
subgraph lay inside and met the chosen cycle, then either it would
contain a path joining two vertices of the cycle, producing a smaller
cycle, or it would contain a tree with an interior leaf, impossible since
all interior vertices have valence four and all leaves of
\(\Gamma(M)\) lie on the boundary of the disk."""
new = r"""Choose one which is innermost: the disk bounded by it contains no other
cycle.  It in fact contains no edge of \(\Gamma(M)\).  Indeed, any
component of the graph lying in that disk either contains a cycle, which
would be smaller, or is a tree.  Such a tree cannot be wholly interior,
because it would have an interior leaf; and if it attaches to the chosen
cycle in two places, the path between the attachment points gives a
smaller cycle.  Thus it would have to attach in exactly one place, again
forcing an interior leaf.  This is impossible since all interior vertices
have valence four and all leaves of \(\Gamma(M)\) lie on the boundary of
the disk."""
assert old in text
p.write_text(text.replace(old,new))
# update notes similar
pn=Path("/mnt/data/research_notes.tex")
nt=pn.read_text()
oldn = r"""Choose an
innermost cycle.  If there were graph inside its disk, then either a
path between two points of the cycle would create a smaller cycle or a
finite tree branch would have an illegal interior leaf (all interior
vertices have valence \(4\), all true leaves are boundary endpoints).
Thus the cycle itself is a boundary component of a complementary
region, giving a component of \(M\cap\partial\overline R\) with no
marked endpoints, contrary to the definition."""
newn = r"""Choose an
innermost cycle.  If there were graph inside its disk, then a component
inside either contains a smaller cycle or is a tree.  A wholly interior
tree would have an illegal interior leaf; a tree attached to the chosen
cycle in two places gives a smaller cycle together with a subarc of the
chosen cycle; and a tree attached in one place again has an interior
leaf.  Hence the cycle itself is a boundary component of a complementary
region, giving a component of \(M\cap\partial\overline R\) with no
marked endpoints, contrary to the definition."""
assert oldn in nt
pn.write_text(nt.replace(oldn,newn))
print("updated")

**Examining potential issues**

I need to think about any hidden issues in my final answer. Since the critique accepted my previous work, that’s reassuring, but I don’t want to be complacent. The question about whether F_w is contractible could mean something specific related to reducible quasi-reduced words. I may need to provide a counterexample. I should also check if the orientation in the context of F_w could affect its homeomorphism to S^2. A deeper mathematical audit seems necessary to ensure I’m avoiding any problems.

**Analyzing crossings and components**

I'm considering whether our drawn diagram with crossings means that Γ might not actually be a forest. If two chords cross, while it seems like there could be a tree structure, the presence of crossings makes things more complicated. Every component of M∩∂R might need to be an arc connecting boundary endpoints. I'm thinking about a situation where two intervals cross, forming an X-shaped tree. Here, I realize that the boundary’s topology is crucial, as it leads to a "V" shape instead of a true 1-manifold.

**Understanding boundaries and connections**

I'm analyzing a connected subset with a boundary that includes two marked endpoints. The topological boundary of a "V" graph has three vertices, but the valence at the crossing is 2, so that's not a boundary. Thus, A is an arc with a corner at the crossing. In a forest, the components of M∩∂R follow corners at crossings. They can't branch since each region occupies a quadrant with two half-edges. The endpoints of the arcs must be leaves. The parity argument suggests that an embedded arc could connect endpoints while splitting the disk into two components without crossing.

**Refining interval considerations**

I'm thinking about what happens if an interval has its endpoint exactly at i or j. It seems like we should consider the intervals strictly between those endpoints. Since i and j are on the boundary of the cut, they shouldn't be counted as sides. Matching intervals can incidentally involve i or j, but the parity needs to be refined. The pushed-off arc endpoints are near i and j, potentially cutting the boundary into arcs, ensuring that intervals align correctly. Excluding i and j helps keep the endpoint counts even within their respective sides.

**Understanding parity and endpoints**

This means that the number of endpoints strictly between i and j along each boundary arc must be even. Since the total is 6, both intervals are equivalent. When i and j are in cyclic order from 1 to 6, they must have opposite parity for the count strictly between them to be even. If we verify, with endpoints i < j, the count equals j - i - 1. If that’s even, then j - i is odd, confirming opposite parity. 

I realize that we need to clarify that the pushed-off arc connecting boundaries near i and j may include that segment. By adjusting i and j to be adjacent, we can keep them out of either side of the cut, which is a solid approach!

**Exploring interleaving graph issues**

I'm thinking about potential issues with the interleaving graph being a forest. Pairing with three pairs excludes all-crossings and any edges connecting an interval to itself, which I don't think should be the case. I wonder if the all-crossing pairing is unique, like \(14|25|36\). With endpoints on a circle, all pairs could interleave. For six cyclic points, perfect matching occurs at opposite vertices, such as (1,4), (2,5), and (3,6). However, I need to ensure that crossing scenarios don’t form triangular cycles since that would complicate things with pairwise crossings.

**Examining intervals and crossings**

I'm looking at three immersed intervals where each pair intersects. There are two crossing vertices along each interval, and the subarcs between them form a cycle of length 3, which seems good. However, I wonder about the isotopy class determined by endpoint pairing and whether the order of crossings matters. For a configuration like 13|25|46, I see how crossings occur. The middle interval crosses both others, but I need to determine if swapping the order would create cycles. It seems the endpoint pairing and forest structure may dictate how careful I need to be with enumeration.

**Analyzing interval matchings and crossings**

I'm thinking about matchings that consist of n intervals embedded without triple intersections. The isotopy classes of k-crossing matchings might not always show minimal chord diagrams. While the forest structure helps exclude extra crossings, a fixed endpoint pairing with a crossing graph can produce non-isotopic configurations due to the order of crossings on the middle interval. For intervals A, B, and C, where B crosses both A and C, the crossing order along B might depend on cyclic endpoint order. I'll test the pairing of 13 (A), 25 (B), and 46 (C), with A and C being disjoint. When crossing from B's endpoints, A should come before C in a standard arrangement.

**Examining crossing orders of intervals**

I'm considering whether interval B could cross interval C first, then A, while keeping A and C disjoint without forming any cycles. If I graph it, B starts at boundary point 2 inside region A. A chord connects points 1 and 3, placing point 2 on the segment between 1 and 3, with point 5 outside. To travel from 2 to 5, B would need to cross A an odd number of times. If C is disjoint from A, it remains outside, so B must cross A first, then C. I realize swapping the order would lead to an impossible scenario. It seems the endpoint pairing does dictate the crossing order. For one crossing cases, it’s straightforward, while noncrossing pairings may result in unique configurations, perhaps related to Catalan arcs.

**Clarifying isotopy classes in diagrams**

I'm thinking about how nested arcs can lead to unique isotopy classes with regard to the boundary, especially if there are no crossings. In a forest graph's planar embedding, the endpoints might also define a unique structure. I want to ensure the answer reflects that, so I consider adding a phrase like, "for these forest diagrams, the planar isotopy class is determined..." My current response states that the crossing pattern is influenced by the endpoint pairing but lacks clarity regarding isotopy classes. 

To improve clarity, I can state that for a forest embedded in a disk with fixed boundary leaves and crossing pairings, there's only one isotopy class. This way, I believe I can make the concept more understandable.

**Examining regular neighborhoods in forests**

I'm considering if a regular neighborhood of a planar forest can be defined as a disk with marked boundary leaves in a specific cyclic order. I think a forest diagram with fixed endpoint pairing and crossing data might indeed be unique up to isotopy relative to the boundary. I wonder if a regular neighborhood could truly be unique in this way.

**Analyzing cyclic orders in planar trees**

I’m thinking that while planar trees can show different cyclic orders at their internal vertices or crossings, the cyclic order of half-edges at these crossings isn’t random. It’s fixed because two opposite half-edges come from the same interval, and crossings have alternating half-edges. I wonder if there could be a mirror image, but with a fixed disk orientation, local crossings don’t allow for that.

The cyclic order at each crossing is determined, and pairs of intervals dictate the crossing's local order through the incoming endpoint arrangements, while the global cyclic order is set by the endpoints. **Examining topological arguments**

The two square cells share that path via edges e01 and e13, but they don’t seem to share any other boundary. Q12’s boundary is P1∪P2, and Q14’s is P1∪P4, so the shared boundary is P1.

old = r"""Hence the crossing
pattern is determined by the endpoint pairing: two pairs cross exactly
when their endpoints interleave around the boundary.

Conversely, take any pairing for which the interleaving graph is a
forest, and draw it with one transverse crossing for each interleaving
pair and no other crossings."""
new = r"""Hence the crossing
pattern is determined by the endpoint pairing: two pairs cross exactly
when their endpoints interleave around the boundary.  There is no
further isotopy datum here; a regular neighbourhood of the resulting
planar forest is unique once the cyclic order of its six boundary leaves
is fixed.

Conversely, take any pairing for which the interleaving graph is a
forest, and draw it with one transverse crossing for each interleaving
pair and no other crossings."""
assert old in text
p.write_text(text.replace(old,new))
pn=Path("/mnt/data/research_notes.tex")
nt=pn.read_text()
oldn = r"""Together with mod-2 crossing parity for chords in a disk, this means
the crossing pattern is determined by the endpoint pairing: two pairs
cross iff their endpoints interleave.  The unique all-crossing pairing
is \(14|25|36\), so it is excluded."""
newn = r"""Together with mod-2 crossing parity for chords in a disk, this means
the crossing pattern is determined by the endpoint pairing: two pairs
cross iff their endpoints interleave.  In these forest cases there is
no extra isotopy datum; the regular neighbourhood of the forest is
unique once the cyclic order of the six boundary leaves is fixed.  The
unique all-crossing pairing is \(14|25|36\), so it is excluded."""
assert oldn in nt
pn.write_text(nt.replace(oldn,newn))
print("done")

content.text:
Updated the canonical files. I made minor exposition refinements to the forest/innermost-cycle argument, the isotopy uniqueness point, and the final \(S^2\) identification.

`pdflatex` ran successfully on `answer.tex`; the PDF is 3 pages.
